english: as ground-colour in staining houses with dkár-rtsi (dkar rtsi) Ld.
n. of a holy Lama, teacher of Milaraspa, by whom he was highly respected.

the place where a thing is, its site, situation, *mál-du żág-pa* (mal du zhag pa) C. *bór-če* (bor ce) W., to put a thing in its own place; also where a thing has been, its trace, vestige, šiṅ-rtai (shing rtai) rut, wheel-mark, track; mal gčig-tu _o_dúg-pa (mal gcig tu 'dug pa) prob. to be unstable, changeable, fickle, restless; more esp. place of rest, couch, bed, mál-gyi _o_g-tu (mal gyi 'og tu) under the bed Glr.; dgoṅs-mal (dgongs mal) resp. for night-quarters Dzl. 72, 3 (so acc. to the xylographic copy; Sch. having the less appropriate dgoṅs-lam (dgongs lam)); *mal dúg-če* (mal dug ce) W. to live in a strange place, ἐπιδημεῖν; mal bdé-ba (mal bde ba) Sch. a quiet sleep, ṅai lus sems mál-du bde (ngai lus sems mal du bde) I now may safely lie down, fig. for: the danger is now over Glr. — mal-kŕi (mal khri) bed-frame, bed-stead. — mal-gós (mal gos) Cs., mal-čá (mal cha) Lex., *mal-čé* (mal che) C., *-stán* (stan) C., W.. Dzl. bedding, bed-clothes. — mal-ldaṅ (mal ldang) Sch. 'cradle', rather improb., perh. hammock. — mal-yól (mal yol) bed-curtain. — mál-sa (mal sa), resp. gzims-mál (gzims mal) couch, bed.
Ssk., properly name of a flower, Jasminum Champaca, used as an epithet in pompous titles of books.

1. instrum. case of ma (ma) mother. — 2. v. ma (ma) II, the lower part, gen. however with terminative meaning, downward, towards the lower parts, mas btáṅ-ba (mas btang ba) Med. to move downward, to purge; backward, last Sch.; used also as a sbst.: más-kyi (mas kyi) the last, e.g. yi-ge (yi ge) final letter Cs.; más-la (mas la) downward, below Sch., más-nas (mas nas) from below Sch.; cf. the contrary yás (yas).

I. num. figure: 46. — II. sbst. man, mi gsod-pa (mi gsod pa) to kill men, to murder, mi-méd ri-kŕód (mi med ri khrod) uninhabited, desolate mountains Mil.; mi-rnams ná-re (mi rnams na re) people said Mil.; mi-la ma lab (mi la ma lab) tell no body else of it Mil; ráṅ-gis bságs-pa mi-yis spyod (rang gis bsags pa mi yis spyod) what we gathered ourselves, is enjoyed by others Mil.; mi-nor raṅ sloṅ-ba (mi nor rang slong ba) to gather by begging what belongs to others Mil.; mii bú-mo (mi'i bu mo) 1. daughters of men, opp. to lhai bú-mo (lha'i bu mo) e.g. witches appear in the shape of daughters of men Mil. 2. daughters of others, opp. to ráṅ-gi bú-mo (rang gi bu mo) Mil., cf. also mi-bu (mi bu) further on; pleon. before a pers. pron. of the first person: mi-ṅá (mi nga), mi-bdág (mi bdag) I, Mil. (cf. po (po)), and with certain sbst.: gtsó-bo mi drug (gtso bo mi drug) (we) six lords Glr.; plur. also mi-tsố (mi tsho) Sch. Comp. mi-k'a (mi kha), (idle) talk of the people, common talk, yúl-sdei náṅ-nas mi-k'a sdud (yul sde'i nang nas mi kha sdud) in the whole neighbourhood one is an object of gossip, nif.; defaming talk; imprecating speech, with or without ṅán-pa (ngan pa), mi-k'á zug (mi kha zug) or pog (phog) (damnation) lights on (me, him) Dom. — mi-kyim (mi khyim) 1. human dwelling, house, (the Chinese capital contained) mi-kyim _o_búm-tso (mi khyim 'bum tsho) 100 000 houses Glr. 2. Ld.-Glr. Schl. 20. b. and Glr. 94, 7 it seems to imply the people of a household, domestics, the same as kyím-gái mi (khyim gyi mi). — mi-grén (mi gren) v. grén-ba (gren ba). — mi-rgód (mi rgod) v. rgód-pa (rgod pa) II. — mi-brgyúd (mi brgyud) v. brgyud (brgyud). — mi-rjé (mi rje) sovereign, king, mi-rjé mdzád-pa (mi rje mdzad pa) to be king, to reign Glr. — mi-nyíd (mi nyid) Cs: 'humanity, honesty'; mi-nyíd-čan (mi nyid can) 'humane, honest' (?) — mi-brdág (mi brdag) 1. = mi-rjé (mi rje). 2. symb. num.: 16. — mi-mda (mi mda) (vulgo mín-da (min da)) Mil. and C., W.: men, persons preceded by a numeral, e.g. six men, six women (prop. a line or row of people). — mi-sdé (mi sde) v. sde (sde). Sch. has also: lha-sdé mi-sdé (lha sde mi sde) princes and nations. — mi-sná (mi sna) 1. race of men, class of people (seldom). 2. messenger, delegate, not frq met with in books, yet not unknown in C. and W., and used esp. of messengers with an errand or
